coredata.dc:description: In this work, using discarded white pomelo peel as the green precursor and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as the dopant, water-soluble Cl–N-doped carbon dots (Cl-NCDs) with excellent performance were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The Cl-NCDs function as both luminescent agents and catalysts, significantly enhancing the chemiluminescence intensity of the HCl–HCHO–KMnO4 system, forming a novel HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 chemiluminescence system. The addition of erythromycin (EM) solution inhibits the chemiluminescence intensity of new system. By optimizing the conditions, we established a simple, cost-effective, and reliable chemiluminescence method for EM detection, offering a linear range of 5–100 μM and a detection limit of 0.0149 μM. In addition, this method was successfully applied to the analysis of EM in various water simples, achieving recovery rates ranging from 97.6 % to 106 %.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, using discarded white pomelo peel as the green precursor and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as the dopant, water-soluble Cl–N-doped carbon dots (Cl-NCDs) with excellent performance were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The Cl-NCDs function as both luminescent agents and catalysts, significantly enhancing the chemiluminescence intensity of the HCl–HCHO–KMnO4 system, forming a novel HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 chemiluminescence system. The addition of erythromycin (EM) solution inhibits the chemiluminescence intensity of new system. By optimizing the conditions, we established a simple, cost-effective, and reliable chemiluminescence method for EM detection, offering a linear range of 5–100 μM and a detection limit of 0.0149 μM. In addition, this method was successfully applied to the analysis of EM in various water simples, achieving recovery rates ranging from 97.6 % to 106 %.

A novel chemiluminescence (CL) sensor was successfully developed based on chlorine doped carbon dots (Cl-NCDs), which were synthesized via a one hydrothermal method using discarded white pomelo peel as the green carbon source and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as the chlorine source.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Erythromycin (EM) belongs to the macrolide antibiotics, which have excellent antibacterial activity and are often widely used in clinical medicine, animal husbandry, food industry, and other fields [1–6]. However, abuse of erythromycin can cause vomiting, diarrhea, liver damage, deafness, and other diseases [7,8]. On the other hand, because of its unique lactone ring structure, degradation is challenging in environments like water bodies and soils [9,10]. Thus, the EM detection is of particular concern to protect human health and the environment.

At present, there are several methods to detect Erythromycin, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [1], spectroscopy [8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], electrochemistry [12], capillary electrophoresis [13] and chemiluminescence (CL) [14]. In particular, CL is independent of an external excitation light source and solely utilizes the variations in light signal intensity generated during the chemical reaction to detect and analyze target substances. Luminescent reagents play a crucial role in the practical analytical determination, and standard CL systems mainly include the Luminol system, the KMnO4 system, the Lucigenin system, the peroxy oxalate system, and the sulfite system. Owing to its simplicity, convenient operation, absence of background inference from light sources, and high detection sensitivity [15], this method has garnered significant attention in the fields of detection and analysis, particularly in the testing of drugs, including amoxicillin [16], sulfonamide [17], salicylic acid [18], and gefitinib [19].

In recent years, nanomaterials have become the focus of attention and developed rapidly. A novel class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), with diameters less than 10 nm, are primarily composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen [20]. Besides, nearly all carbon-based materials found in nature can serve as precursors for synthesizing CDs. In previous studies, researchers have carried out in-depth research on CDs synthesis to look forward to renewable, easily accessible, environmental-friendly, and cost-effective green carbon sources, among which the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using biomass waste as a green carbon source has been more and more widely recognized [21]. The carbon framework, chemical structure, and band gap of CDs can be tuned by atomic doping during the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs to improve their optical properties [22]. Therefore, carbon dots have low toxicity, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, and photoluminescence ability [23], which make them widely used in drug delivery [24], fluorescent probes [25], bioimaging [26], sensing [27], catalysis [28], solar cells [29], supercapacitors [30], and other fields. However, there are limited studies on applying biomass waste as a green carbon source and combining CDs and chemiluminescence after regulation by atomic doping.

Rich in various nutrients and functional compounds, pomelo (Citrus maxima (Burm.) Merr) belongs to the family Rutaceae, one of the most consumed fruits, and is widely cultivated worldwide [31]. Pomelos are typically consumed solely for their pulp, with the peel accounting for approximately 55 % of its weight. This results in a considerable amount of pomelo peel being discarded as a byproduct. Pomelo peels also contain numerous active ingredients and are highly perishable if improperly handled. A substantial quantity of discarded pomelo peels represents a waste of resources and a source of environmental pollution. Therefore, to properly harness waste in an eco-friendly way to create value.

In this work, water-soluble Cl–N-doped carbon dots (Cl-NCDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using discarded white pomelo peel as the green carbon source and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as the chlorine source, providing an environmentally friendly and sustainable approach. The addition of Cl-NCDs to the HCl–HCHO–KMnO4 system significantly enhanced the CL intensity. In contrast, the introduction of EM inhibited the CL intensity of the (Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 system. This phenomenon led to the development a novel CL method for the rapid detection of EM.

White pomelo peel was collected from the fruit store of Chongqing Normal University. KMnO4, HCHO, C12H22O11, NaHCO3, (NH4)2HPO4 were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. HCl and H3PO4 were purchased from Chongqing Wansheng Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. EM was purchased from Maya Reagent Co., Ltd. CaCl2 was sourced from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Co., Ltd. NH4Cl was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. KCl was purchased from Tianjin Beichen Fangzheng Reagent Factory. Starch was purchased from Sichuan Pengxian Junle Chemical Factory. C6H12O6 was obtained from Changde Bkmam Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. C6H8O7∙H2O was acquired from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. l-lysine (L-Lys), l-tryptophan (L-Try), ascorbic acid (AA), thiourea (TU), and nitro tetrazolium chloride blue (NBT) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Above all, reagents were analytical grade, and ultrapure water was used during the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Cl-NCDs were scanned using the FEI Tecnai G2 F30 TEM device manufactured by FEI Corporation in the United States. The elemental composition and functional groups of NCDs and Cl-NCDs were analyzed by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer from the United States Thermo Fisher at source HV = 1486.6 eV. The elemental composition and functional groups of NCDs and Cl-NCDs were analyzed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer from Thermo Fisher, United States, at an excitation source HV = 1486.6 eV. XRD diffraction tests on NCDs and Cl-NCDs were performed using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer from Bruker, Germany, at a tub current of 40 mA and a tube voltage of 40 kV, respectively. The Infrared spectroscopy data for NCDs and Cl-NCDs were obtained using the Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer manufactured by Nicolet, Inc. in the United States. The defect extent of NCDs versus Cl-NCDs was analyzed using a Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained by the TU-1810DSPC ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer produced by Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd. and the F-7100 spectrometer of Hitachi Company in Tokyo, Japan, respectively. The CL intensity at a high pressure of 700 V was recorded using a flow injection chemiluminescence meter produced by Xi'an Rimage Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCDs、Cl-NCDs、P-NCDs and P, Cl-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh white pomelo peel was cut into pieces, washed with ultrapure water 3–5 times, dried, and ground into powder. NCDs and Cl-NCDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method. 3.0 g of white pomelo peel was added into ultra-pure H2O (50 mL) and sonicated for 40 min. Next, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 8 h to obtain NCDs solution. At the same time, 1 mL HCl and 1 mL H3PO4 could be directly added to the sonicated solution, respectively, and other reaction conditions are kept unchanged so as to obtain the Cl-NCDs, P-NCDs, and P, Cl-NCDs solution. After natural cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da). The final solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

Fig. 1 displays the CL detection system for EM. The primary pump 1 is used to deliver HCl, HCHO, Cl-NCDs, and KMnO4 solutions, while secondary pump 2 is used to deliver ultrapure water or EM. Besides, the photovoltaic high voltage is set to 700 V, and the rotational speed flow rates of P1 and P2 are the same. The four solutions sequentially enter the pathway through P1 and converge at V1. After reacting with the ultrapure water or EM solution to be tested at V2, it flows through the photomultiplier tube (PMT) to generate an apparent CL intensity. When P2 is connected to ultrapure water and EM, respectively, the CL intensity is recorded as I0 and I. Then, it can calculate the relative CL intensity:
△ I = I ‐
I 0

The lake water is collected from Sanchun Lake at Chongqing Normal University (CQNU), and Longpai mineral water is purchased from the school water supermarket. The lake water samples obtained contained neither solid silt nor visible floating solid material. The trademarks of the mineral water indicate the presence of Ca2+, Na+, K+. Common ions in lake water include Ca2+, Na+, K+, Cl−, and HCO3 −, while those in tap water are mainly Ca2+, Na+, K+, and Cl−. We measured the pH of the lake water, tap water and mineral water at 7.97, 7.74 and 6.26, respectively. All water samples were used at room temperature (25 °C). The detailed information regarding the tested water samples has been incorporated into Table S1.

Fig. 2 a and b demonstrate the TEM morphology of Cl-NCDs under the scale bar od 50 nm and 20 nm. Cl-NCDs show a near-spherical morphology and uniform dispersion in ultrapure water without significant agglomeration. Cl-NCDs were synthesized from discarded white pomelo peel. A clear lattice structure with a lattice spacing of about 0.23 nm is observed from the HR-TEM image of Cl-NCDs (Fig. 2c), which is attributed to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, and at the same time indicates that the graphite structure of Cl-NCDs is produced with good crystallization properties [32]. The particle size distribution of Cl-NCDs is calculated after randomly measuring 170 Cl-NCDs (Fig. 2d), which shows that the particle size of Cl-NCDs is mainly distributed in the range of 1.29–4.51 nm, with an average particle size of 2.7 nm. There is a wide variety of precursors for CDs. Table 1 provides a detailed comparison of the CDs synthesis routes for several different precursors and their corresponding sizes, revealing the relationships and differences between the various preparation methods. It demonstrates that the hydrothermal method is suitable for natural plant sources and chemical reagent precursors. The size of the prepared CDs is concentrated in the range of 1.00–6.00 nm. In contrast, the size of CDs prepared by the gluconeogenic calcination method can reach up to 10.30 ± 0.63 nm. This implies that the differences in the chemical structures of the different precursors and the synthesis routes have an impact on the size and other properties of the CDs. The reaction temperature of the hydrothermal method ranges from 190 °C to 400 °C, and the reaction time ranges from 3 to 20 h, indicating that different combinations of reaction temperature and time exert an influence on the preparation of CDs.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of NCDs and Cl-NCDs, both showing a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23°, which is related to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, indicating a typical graphite structure [41]. The diffraction peaks of Cl-NCDs are more significant than those of NCDs, which may be due to the doping of the Cl element. In order to further verify the graphitized structure of Cl-NCDs and NCDs, the structural defects were analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3b), and there is a prominent absorption peak around 1373.57 cm−1, which corresponds to the typical D band on carbon nanomaterials, reflecting the disorder or defect of the sp3 carbon atoms [34]. A distinct peak near 1553.27 cm−1 corresponds to the G-band of carbon nanomaterials, indicating graphitized sp2 carbon [34]. ID/IG is often used to assess the relative contribution of graphite core states and disordered surface states to the structure [42]. The ID/IG of Cl-NCDs and the NCDs are 0.80 and 0.90, respectively. The ID/IG of Cl-NCDs is smaller compared to NCDs, indicating that the presence of defects on the surface and NCDs have a high degree of defects and a low degree of graphitization. Therefore, doping with the Cl atom can change the degree of defects in the original NCDs. The XRD and Raman results agree with the analytical result of HRTEM.

The surface functional groups of NCDs and Cl-NCDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 3c, the FTIR spectra of NCDs and Cl-NCDs show that NCDs and Cl-NCDs have prominent peaks around 2924 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1, attributed to the tensile vibration of the C–H bond [35]. The peak of NCDs at 1670 cm−1 and Cl-NCDs at 1700 cm−1 are related to CO/CC [43], while there is a significant peak at 3284 cm−1, which the tensile vibration of and N–H may generate [44]. The peaks appearing at 1019 cm−1 and 1278 cm−1 for NCDs and 1022 cm−1 and 1160 cm−1 for Cl-NCDs are attributed to C–O and C–N vibrations [35,44,45]. The peak of Cl-NCDs at 800 cm−1 is due to the C–Cl stretching vibration, which indicates that the Cl atom has been successfully doped into the NCDs [46]. Besides, the peak of NCDs at 1394 cm−1 and Cl-NCDs at 1398 cm−1 are related to the presence of C–N and –COOH [47,48]. The results of FTIR analysis illustrate the functional groups covered on the surface of Cl-NCDs, and the preparation of Cl-NCDs has been accomplished.

The XPS results illustrate that NCDs mainly include C, N and O, with the proportions of 69.01 %, 27.32 % and 3.67 %, respectively. Cl-NCDs are mainly composed of C, N, O, and Cl, which account for 69.12 %, 27.54 %, 2.77 % and 0.57 % of the Cl-NCDs. The XPS spectra of NCDs and Cl-NCDs (Fig. 4 a) shows the presence of three typical peaks: O 1s (533.08 eV), N 1s (400.08 eV), and C 1s (286.08 eV), with one peak at 198.08 eV, corresponding to Cl 2p [49], indicating that Cl has been successfully doped into Cl-NCDs. Meanwhile, the two peaks at 197.68 eV and 199.38 eV in the high-resolution Cl 2p spectra of Cl-NCDs (Fig. 4b) correspond to COO–Cl and C–Cl, respectively [50]. Fig. 4c shows the high-resolution C 1s spectra of NCDs, with three peaks at 284.78 eV, 286.28 eV, and 288.98 eV, attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO, respectively [35,45]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra of NCDs (Fig. 4e) was fitted with two peaks, 531.88 eV and 532.98 eV, attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C [51], respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectra of NCDs (Fig. 4g) had four peaks, 399.78 eV, 400.18 eV, 401.68 eV, and 406.5 eV, attributed to C–N–C, N-(C)3, N–H, and –NO3, respectively [51,52]. However, in the high-resolution C1s spectra of Cl-NCDs (Fig. 4d), the four peaks belong to the following distinct functional groups C–C/CC (284.88 eV) [43,53], C–O/C–N (286.18 eV) [54], C–Cl (287.75 eV) [30], and CO/COOH (288.98 eV) [25,54], respectively, and the 532.08 eV and 532.78 eV peaks in the high-resolution O 1s spectra in Fig. 4f, corresponding to CO and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively [53]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 4h) fit four characteristic peaks at 399.68 eV, 400.28 eV, 401.78 eV, and 406.78 eV, indicating that the N element of Cl-NCDs species exists mainly in four forms: C–N–C、N-(C)3、N–H and –NO3 [45,51]. The XPS analysis is consistent with the FTIR results, suggesting that the Cl atom was successfully doped into NCDs, and its surface functional groups contained –OH and –COOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different atomic doping on the CL intensity of NCDs were investigated. Fig. 5 a demonstrates the CL intensities of NCDs, P-NCDs, Cl-NCDs, and P, Cl-NCDs (180 °C, 4 h) in the acidic potassium permanganate system, and the results suggest that the CL intensity of Cl-NCDs in the acidic potassium permanganate system is the most significant. It also confirms that P, Cl atoms are successfully doped into NCDs and can somewhat enhance the CL intensity. At the same time, the effects of Cl doping concentration on the CL intensity of NCDs were also investigated. Fig. S1 demonstrates the alteration in CL intensity of Cl-NCDs as a function of the volume of HCl used for doping. Notably, the peak CL intensity of Cl-NCDs was detected when the doping volume of HCl reached 1.0 mL.

The temperature and time of hydrothermal heating determine, to some extent, the size and morphology of NCDs, which in turn affects their optical properties [36,55]. Therefore, the effects of different temperatures and times on the CL intensity of Cl-NCDs were investigated using a one-way experimental approach.

The reaction time was set to 4 h, and the temperature varied from 160 °C to 190 °C. As shown in Fig. 5b, the CL intensity of Cl-NCDs gradually enhances with the reaction temperature increase. It reaches the maximum when the temperature increases to 180 °C and decreases after exceeding 180 °C. Therefore, Cl-NCDs are prepared at 180 °C.

Fig. 5c demonstrates the change in the CL intensity of Cl-NCDs by hydrothermal heating at different times at 180 °C. With the increase in reaction time, the CL intensity of Cl-NCDs gradually enhances, and the highest CL intensity is obtained by hydrothermal heating for 8 h. Thus, the hydrothermal time of Cl-NCDs is determined to be 8 h. The above results show that the CL intensity of Cl-NCDs prepared by hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 8 h was the most significant after adding 1.0 mL HCl as a dopant.

The CL intensity of HCl–HCHO–KMnO4 and HCl–HCHO–KMnO4-(Cl-NCDs) was investigated under the same high pressure and amplification stages, and the results showed that the addition of Cl-NCDs could significantly enhance the CL intensity of the KMnO4 system (Fig. 5d).

Cl-NCDs’ optical properties were investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5e, the absorption peak at 220 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectra corresponds to the electronic transition of the π-π∗ of the CC bond, and the peaks at 260 nm and 320–350 nm are attributed to the n-π∗ of the CO bond [34,43,47]. Fig. 5f shows the fluorescence emission spectra of Cl-NCDs at different excitation wavelengths. Adjusting the excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 420 nm with a change of every 10 nm, an increase of the emission peak wavelength from 390 nm to 490 nm can be observed, which indicates that Cl-NCDs have a typical excitation-dependent emission behavior. Meanwhile, the emission intensity first increases and then decreases; the maximum emission peak is located at 400 nm, and the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum value at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. And the fluorescence emission spectra of Cl-NCDs were measured at 530 nm close to the Raman excitation wavelength (Fig. S2), the maximum emission peak is located at 545 nm. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Cl-NCDs and NCDs are determined to be 3.19 % and 0.62 % using quinine sulfate as the reference material. From the results, it is clear that the fluorescence emission behavior of Cl-NCDs is improved.

Furthermore, the effect of pH on fluorescence stability was investigated in the rage of 1–9 (Fig. S3). The Cl-NCDs showed the lower fluorescence under the strong acidity conditions, while exhibited the remarkable fluorescence intensity when pH was neutral (pH = 6–9). This may be attributed to the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of Cl-NCDs, and hydrogen bonds are easily formed in the strong acidic environment, while H+ is neutralized in the alkaline condition [51,52].

HCl provides an acidic environment for the system, so it is imperative to select the appropriate concentration of HCl. The effect of HCl at a concentration of 0.1 M–1.2 M on the CL intensity was investigated under the conditions of 50-fold dilution of Cl-NCDs solution, 6 % HCHO, 1000 μM concentration of KMnO4 and 1000 μM concentration of erythromycin. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CL intensity of the system gradually enhances with the increase of HCl concentration, and the CL intensity of the system is most significant when the HCl concentration is 1.0 M. Therefore, the optimal concentration of HCl is 1.0 M.

HCHO is often used as a sensitizer in acidic potassium permanganate systems, which plays a synergistic role with Cl-NCDs to enhance the CL intensity of the system and then optimizes the HCHO concentration in the order of HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4. The effect of HCHO solution at a concentration of 1%–10 % on the CL intensity was investigated when the HCl concentration was set to 1 M, the dilution factor of Cl-NCDs solution was 50, the concentration of KMnO4 was 1000 μM (Fig. 6b), and the CL intensity of the system gradually decreased when the HCHO concentration was more than 6 %. Thus, 6 % is the optimal concentration of HCHO.

Fixing the concentrations of HCl and HCHO as the optimal concentrations and the erythromycin concentration was 1000 μM, Fig. 6c demonstrates the change in the CL intensity of the Cl-NCDs solution in the acidic potassium permanganate system after a 50-fold dilution to a 100-fold dilution. The CL intensity of the system appears to increase and then decreases with the increase of the dilution time. When the NCDs factor is 85, the maximum CL intensity of the system is reached. Therefore, the optimal dilution of the Cl-NCDs solution is 85-fold.

KMnO4, as an oxidant, is an essential factor affecting the CL of this system. When the dilution of HCl, HCHO and Cl-NCDs reach the optimal state, and the erythromycin concentration is 1000 μM, Fig. 6d demonstrates the change of CL intensity of HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 system when the KMnO4 concentration of 400–1000 μM. The results show that when the concentration of KMnO4 is 800 μM, the CL intensity of the system reach the maximum, and the CL intensity shows a decreasing trend when exceeding this point. Thus, 800 μM is the optimal concentration for KMnO4 solution.

The HCl, HCHO and KMnO4 solution concentrations are 1 M, 6 % and 800 μM, respectively, and the Cl-NCDs solution is diluted 85 times. EM solution is added to HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4, and it is observed that the EM solution effectively inhibited the chemiluminescence intensity of the system, and its linear range was 5–100 μM. The EM concentration (C) and the relative luminescence intensity (△I) showed a good linear relationship, and Fig. 6e was the standard curve. The linear regression equation was △I = 83.629C–350.2, R2 = 0.998; and the RSD was 1.10 % for 11 parallel measurements of the EM standard solution at a concentration of 40 μM, and the LOD was 0.0149 μM and the LQD was 0.0495 μM (LOD = 3SD/K, LQD = 10SD/K). The results of other methods for EM detection are shown in Table 2 . As can be seen from the table, this detection method has a low LOD.

Interference from other substances is also essential in selectivity and anti-interference performance [19]. When the concentration of EM is 60 μM, the soluble starch, anhydrous glucose, sucrose, citric acid monohydrate, common metal cations, and anions at different concentrations are detected without interference in Fig. 6f: 1000 times of anhydrous glucose, sucrose, citric acid monohydrate, HCO3 −、Na+、Ca2+、K+, starch; 500 times PO4 2−、NH4 +、H+、Cl−; and 50 times L-Lys do not interfere with the detection system. Above all, it illustrates the excellent stability of the newly established HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 system.

In order to evaluate the applicability of this method, lake water (pH = 7.97), tap water (pH = 7.74), and mineral water (pH = 6.26) were collected and spiked. After the lake water samples were left to stand for 7 days, the supernatant was ultrasonicated for 40 min and then filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane multiple times for use. Tap water samples and mineral water samples were also ultrasonicated and filtered to ensure that no air bubbles remain in the flow injection chemiluminescence system. The same sample (V = 20 mL) is measured in parallel 3 times, and the average value is calculated with a recovery rate of 97.6%–106 %, as shown in Table 3 , which proves that the established method is feasible and can be successfully applied to the determination of EM content in actual samples. All experiments were performed at room temperature (25 °C).

The UV–Vis patterns of KMnO4 solution, KMnO4-(Cl-NCDs), KMnO4–HCHO solution, and KMnO4-(Cl-NCDs)-HCHO solution were investigated by ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Fig. 7 a shows a significant absorption peak at 530 nm for an 800 μM KMnO4 solution (curve 2). The absorption peak at 540 nm decreases significantly after adding Cl-NCDs to HCl, which provides an acidic environment for the KMnO4 solution (curve 3). When the acidic KMnO4 solution coexists with the HCHO solution, the peak value at 540 nm decreases, implying that a redox reaction has occurred to form Mn (III), which reduces Mn (III) to Mn (II)∗. The absorption peak near 450 nm (curve 4) may be attributed to the permanganate intermediate formed during the oxidation of HCHO [60,61]. When acidic KMnO4 solution, HCHO solution, and Cl-NCDs exist at the same time, the peak at 450 nm disappears, the overall trend of the curve remains unchanged, and the absorption peak of Cl-NCDs decreases significantly, confirming the synergistic effect of Cl-NCDs solution and HCHO solution (curve 5). Therefore, the above results indicate that Cl-NCDs can be used as catalysts to accelerate the reaction rate of the system and can as reactants to directly participate in the system's reaction. When EM is detected by KMnO4-(Cl-NCDs)–HCHO–HCl, the characteristic absorption peak of EM solution at 420 nm is significantly reduced (curves 1 and 6), indicating that the chemical reaction of EM solution in KMnO4-(Cl-NCDs)–HCHO–HCl system could significantly inhibit chemiluminescence.

Superoxide anion radicals (•O2 −) and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) can also act as efficient electron injectors for CDs [62–64]. Therefore, in order to better understand the effects of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), •O2 − and single-line oxygen radicals (1O2) on the CL intensity of HCl–HCHO-(Cl-CDs)-KMnO4 system, ascorbic acid (AA), thiourea (TU), nitro tetrazolium chloride blue (NBT) and l-tryptophan (L-Try) at a concentration of 1 mM were used for analysis, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7b, adding AA to the CL system containing Cl-NCDs almost wholly inhibits the CL signal, indicating that reactive oxygen species play a crucial role in CL. At the same time, 1 mM TU and L-Try are added to the system, and it is found that there is a significant quenching effect on the CL signal of the system, indicating that •OH and 1O2 are involved in the CL of the system. When 1 mM NBT is added, the CL intensity of the system is suppressed, and the CL signal decreases, suggesting that •O2 − is released in the CL reaction containing Cl-NCDs. In a word, the HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 system contains •OH, 1O2 and •O2 −.

Moreover, 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), represents a stable free radical species that has been extensively harnessed for the quantification of the antioxidant capacity inherent in reducing Cl-NCDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the absorbance of DPPH at 520 nm gradually decreases when Cl-NCDs are added, indicating that Cl-NCDs have good reducing properties. At the same time, the capacity of Cl-NCDs to generate radicals was ascertained by employing 9,10-Anthracendiyl-bis (methylene) dimalonic (ABDA)and p-Phthalic acid (PTA) as scavengers for 1O2 and •OH radicals, respectively. After the addition of Cl-NCDs to the ABDA solution, it was found that the characteristic absorption peak of ABDA at 399 nm was significantly reduced (Fig. S5), which can indicate that the Cl-NCDs in the solution to be tested have the ability to produce 1O2. According to the fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. S6, when Cl-NCDs were added to the PTA solution, fluorescence enhancement at 442 nm was observed, which can indicate that the Cl-NCDs in the solution to be tested have the ability to produce •OH.

Based on the above analytical results and discussions, combined with the reported light-emitting mechanism of KMnO4 [65,66],the luminescence mechanism of HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)- KMnO4 chemiluminescence system is speculated as shown in Fig. 8 . Cl-NCDs can act as catalysts to participate in the chemiluminescence of the system, so that it can produce more free radicals and accelerate the reduction of Mn (III) to Mn (II)∗ [61]. Meanwhile, Cl-NCDs can directly participate in the system reaction as reactants, which is due to the •OH generated under acidic KMnO4 conditions injecting holes into Cl-NCDs to generate Cl-NCDs•+, and the reducing superoxide anion provides electrons to Cl-NCDs from the group (•O2 −), thus forming Cl-NCDs•-, and its electron transfer annihilation forms excited Cl-NCDs∗ as the final emitter of the HCl–HCHO–Cl-CDs-KMnO4 chemiluminescence system [67–69]. A 1O2 can also directly transfer its own energy to Cl-NCDs∗ [70]. When Cl-NCDs∗ return to the ground state, they release energy and produce an intense photon emission beam, resulting in chemiluminescence.

This work synthesizes water-soluble Cl-NCDs through a one-step hydrothermal process, utilizing discarded white pomelo peel as a green precursor and HCl as a dopant. The Cl-NCDs possessed excellent optical properties and significantly enhanced the chemiluminescence intensity of KMnO4–HCHO–HCl system. This led to the construction of a new HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 chemiluminescence system, where the luminescence mechanism is elaborated, indicating that Cl-NCDs function as both catalysts and luminescent agents. However, the addition of EM could effectively inhibit the chemiluminescence of HCl–HCHO-(Cl-NCDs)-KMnO4 system. Based on this phenomenon, a simple and sensitive EM detection method was developed, achieving a detection limit of 0.0149 μM. This method was successfully applied to the detection of EM in water samples, with recovery rates ranging from 97.6% to 106 %. This study provides a novel direction and evidence for the application of CDs and CL in detecting environmental pollutants and drugs.
